q: In 1678, the Dutch physicist Christiaan Huygens introduced the particle theory of light.
Huygens' principle states that every point on a wavefront may be considered as a source of secondary wavelets.
The new position of a wavefront is the envelope of the secondary wavelets.
Secondary wavelets spread out in all directions with a speed different from the original propagating wave.
A wavefront is defined as a surface associated with a propagating wave which is a locus of all points in the wave that have the same phase.
At a given moment of time, all particles of the medium on a particular wavefront are undergoing the same phase.
Wavefront is always parallel to the direction of propagation (or) rays.
A spherical wavefront from a source transforms into a plane wavefront when it is far away from the source.
The phase angle is the position of a point on a reference circle of a wavefront at an instant.
The angular position of a point of a periodic wave is known as the phase angle.
If phase difference are $0, 2\pi, 4\pi, 6\pi, \dots$, these two positions are completely out of phase.
If phase difference are $\pi, 3\pi, 5\pi, \dots$, these two positions are completely out of phase.
Path difference indicates the distance between two points measured along the direction of propagation of the wave through the medium.
If path difference is equal to $0, \lambda, 2\lambda, 3\lambda, \dots$, the points are said to be in phase.
If path difference are $\frac{\lambda}{2}, \frac{3\lambda}{2}, \frac{5\lambda}{2}, \dots$, it is said completely out of phase.
The relation between phase difference and path difference is expressed as $\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta x$.
In wave optics, ___ principle explained how the light propagates from a light source.
___ light means that it has a single wavelength.
What is the term for the locus of all points in a wave that have the same phase?
According to Huygens' principle, what is the speed of the secondary wavelets in a medium where the propagating wave has speed $v$?
If two positions of periodic waves are 'in phase' (same phase), what is a possible phase difference between them?
The wavefront is always ___ to the direction of propagation.
Which type of wavefront is produced by a source when viewed from a very large distance?
Wave optics (physical optics) deals with the study of interference and diffraction where the ray approximation of geometrical optics is not valid.
Huygens' principle states that the source of secondary wavelets can only be at the center of the source.
